But panelists, so otherwise, was that cool for like the findings from the seven-figure gyms? Okay, awesome. So we're just going to transition. We've perfect. So this is a question about structure and getting the operational and sales manager skin in the game. What do you think of this structure? So they buy in for say 10 or 15 grand, they know. And then they get bonuses based on performance of red, yellow, green. Green being positive revenue over the last year's month, same month. And also cost, less costs from last year's same month. So two positives is a green, one positive, one negative is yellow, two negatives red. What do you think of that? That sounds so complicated. Yeah. I was just, yeah. I mean, like what's happening or what led you to that conclusion? What? I studied, I was a manager at PF Chang's and I studied their structure and I liked that. Honestly, when it comes to like commission structure, I would tie it to like what is their KPI. And so like for someone who's in sales, it's sales rather than like when you go back into tying things to like the profit, like there's so many. I wouldn't do that yet. I wouldn't do that right away. And I wouldn't do that with somebody who's new. Did we have an event this month that cost us money? Did we buy t-shirts this month that aided into profit that you should have bought that they wouldn't buy because they wanted to get their two greens, like things like that, especially with a small business? Like PF Chang's does what, 150,000-ish a month, 200,000 a month for location? Yes. Right. I just, I used to train somebody who was the manager of PF Chang's as well. But like they're, they also have so many systems in place of what they have to spend from corporate that they're kind of boxed into a lot of things. But what I think a couple of people have asked this, I think Jason was asking yesterday about Donna, his fiance, like what should I compensate the operations manager? The reality is that most people who are that type of personality tend to be more security based. They're not the type of people who are, like the biggest lesson that I've learned is that I wanted to structure incentives for each position as though I were hiring myself. But I'm not hiring myself. I'm hiring other people who have different needs than I do. And that was like a huge epiphany for me. And so like an operations manager is going to be more security based. They're going to be more detail-oriented. They're going to be more people, loyalty SC than you're going to be. And so those people are usually very, very happy to just make you happy as long as you've given them that feedback cycle. Like, Lily is amazing at customer service. And it's because of how much she loves the customers and she's bought into the mission. I hope I'm not speaking out of place, Lily. And so I don't think it's, I think you'd be better served just giving someone a good salary and having an amazing onboarding process and having them bought into your culture, which is why I think having clients move up to that is a great way to do it, or friends and family. I just wondered if there were specific appointment windows like Tuesdays or better than Thursdays. I can tell you when we were running the sales team with ACHI's, I would look at tons of data. And when we cut the windows from 3 to 8 or 4 to 8 p.m., we had the same show rates, same number of sales, and it was just more convenient to have everybody work leads and then just sell on those windows. So just for me, when I looked at days of the week across 30 campaigns that we had run in different markets. I had no difference. The only thing that was actually statistically significant is that we had a slightly higher close rate on Fridays, which I think maybe is because if someone showed up on a Friday, they ain't got nothing else to do. Just start right there. There you go. Go ahead. A nutrition question. I have two women that I'm working with. This is their, they're in their fourth week and they're sending me pictures of their food. They say they're 100% compliant, weighing and measuring everything. One of them I believe, the other one I don't. The one I believe is coming off of four and a half months of NutriSystem and I finally peeled that out of her. So when I asked her, you know, because something's not to add enough, she's 40% body fat and her body fat's going up. She's doing, she's 138 pounds. So she's doing 1.25. Um, we trimmed back some of the calories a little bit. Like I took two ounces of protein out, told her to cut her carbs a little bit. She's like doing it. She's still losing muscle. So I got it out of her that she was doing NutriSystem and when she told me the amount of, of the protein that she was doing, I was like, I'm going to lose my muscle. And the amount of macros, it was 37 grams of protein per day, 95 grams of carbs and 15 grams of fat. So my question is, did she just wreck herself prior to doing this and now the only way out is to keep her metabolic rate, you know, her calorie intake higher until she kind of balances out and then cut? What do I tell her? Okay, is this, is this working? Hello? All right. Do you collect whether or not a person has a dieting history before you start them on a six week challenge? Like, are they at the end of another 18 week challenge and then you're starting fresh with a six week challenge? Yeah, so I'm going to start doing that. I did not this with her. No, if I would have known that, I probably would have said, you're like at the bottom of the barrel already. Okay. Yeah. So like I, I love to go back to that, that money in the bank analogy, there's only so much you can take away. Um, I don't think the person has wrecked to themselves per se. Uh, but working them back up to like a normal human amount of food is probably a priority. Getting the person to maintain and manage their expectations so that they can be in a position to take money out of the bank again later is probably the most important part. And, uh, if they want to play the hormone game, like I'm a scientist, right? Take a measurement, ask a question. And then if it does come back wacky, then you can use drugs to fix that. And if you don't want to use drugs to fix that, then this is a numbers game and you have to eat as much as possible to maintain progress and that may or may not be tolerable to you. So at this point, you're probably better off inching calories back up as much as you can to try and maintain a reasonable weight because that sort of weight for a woman, like I'm not entirely sure that's even six week challenge material, right? Is she going to, she's supposed to be a hundred pounds when she's done. She this big? She's a five to one thirty eight forty forty one percent body fat. Okay. So that's something to consider too that a lot of people may not be good six week challengers because they just don't have that much to lose. And when you do get beyond a certain level, it will come from lean tissue, which does end up screwing up the metabolic rate a bit. But if this person was on like a bariatric diet without bariatric surgery, then there's probably a little bit of lean tissue loss. That person needs to train and focusing on the fact that if you just diet, you're just going to be a smaller version of the self you don't like anyway. And that if you incorporate resistance training, that's what changes your shape. She's training four days a week, three to four days. Good. Then feed it. Okay. You can use that language. So should I just tell her, look, for right now, let's not focus on body fat loss. Let's get you, let's get your. And you can use a language to like if you put muscle on the relative amount of body fat will go down, right? If the person has the same amount of body fat and gains muscle mass, the relative amount of fat on the frame will be lower. So ultimately people don't like their shape. Does that what makes sense just to that you call it? Absolutely. Yeah, that makes sense to me. What I'm trying to figure out with her is she wants to lose weight and her body fats go on 41%, 42. And she wants to cut more calories. I'm like, I'm not cutting you anymore. It's not going to work. That's really your prerogative. Honestly, focusing on a fitness goal, like can she do five pull ups? That's probably if you, if a woman can do five pull ups, she's going to look like she can do five pull ups. And ultimately it's, it's a look and a shape and a feeling. And if you don't have any money in the bank, you can't borrow. So I think this is a great opportunity to like instead of saying something like maintenance, say that you can go into recovery and like, so you can, so there's no gain. There's no like gain or there's no maintenance. There's like, what's the language I'm working out is something like there's fat loss recovery and performance, which would be like the equivalent of bulking. So no one purposefully gains, but you call it like cruise time for me. We're going to like cruise mold. So this person, you can, you can use the language this person needs to recover because this is like a, this is like a contractor going to a house to read you the drywall and turn the drywall off and seeing that things are going to catch fire any second because, because the electricity is crappy. Right. So this is something where you, you want to help this person and that you just, you got to feed that beast. Okay. Got it. Yeah. Cool man. Thanks. Next box. Yeah. Over here. I guess this is for Dr. Casci. I have a, I have a couple of body fat scales. I'm using a TANITA right now and I have no way, I don't know rhyme or reason why sometimes I'll measure somebody. I'm going to be like 21% body fat. Two days later, they measure again, same time and they're like 24% body fat. And I've seen all the pictures of what they're eating to follow in the meal plan. Is there any kind of rhyme or reason behind them? I know D height. I've scoured the internet too. So by electrical impedance, like there, there's a bunch of different ways that you can measure body fat, right? And each one has varying levels of, so there's precision and accuracy, right? And the decision is whether it is consistent. Like if you're going to always shoot into the left, but it always shoots to the left, then it's precise, but it's not accurate. Accurate is like whether the true value is right. The, the issue is that the tools that we have at our disposal, like if you could do a dexas game with everyone, that would be awesome. Or if you could do hydrostatic waiting for everyone, it's just most people aren't going to, we're not going to shell out $50 a week every single week for them to get hydrostatically weighed. And so we just have to use the tools that are at our disposal. I purposely used the BMI handheld because I also knew how to game it as a coach. So I knew that if I switched it from normal to athlete, I'd drop like one or two percentage on the test and they would never know. And so if I could get someone to, if I, and like, and this is where, this is like kind of a moral question, but for me, if I could get a, yeah, it totally is. If I could get someone to like keep momentum and not feel like it's a big deal, which is why whenever I had body fat people and just in the trainings, I say, listen, if you're a body fat percentage person, I don't want you to measure it between beginning and end. If they did beginning and then at the end, they're going to, you'll see a significant enough drop, which is also why the last week protocol that I had for people who were doing weight loss versus body fat percentage was different. If you're doing weight loss, pure weight loss, then it's going to be literally the opposite. I'm going to be trying to dehydrate you, cut your salt, things like that so that we can get you into a raisin when you weigh out. Whereas when you were doing the body fat percentage, I'm going to try and trick the machine into making you look super hydrated because it's going to read water as muscle so that it's going to give them a lower percentage. Since we have the green play and a lot of people have already switched to credit only, in the short term, I'd rather have a client success so that they feel momentum and psychological win so that I can then keep their vine and say, cool, we did this. Now let's look at a year because at the end of the day, like, I don't think this, like, there are periods of losing and then there are periods of cruising. Right? Kind of sounding good. But I think to periods of losing and then cruising, but to what I would do with a lot of the people that we had was I would shift their goals from weight loss to performance to weight loss to performance and that worked really well without saying you're maintaining. Yeah, maintaining is a four letter word. Don't use it. Yeah, yeah, yeah, exactly. Because people want to make progress. So, okay, we're going to shift your progress from weight loss towards performance. So, we're going to, like, this is where you're at now, I want to get you here. In the meantime, we're going to keep the nutrition locked in so that we can keep this progress going. And they're like, okay, and then once we get to that point, then we can make another big run here. Make sense? Yeah. You always want them looking at the next thing, but you can kind of, like, slide of hand. That's like, this is so much more coaching than it is science, like biochemistry. My answer for your question would not have been helpful. Yeah. He was like, you should answer. I think something that you should emphasize more, like, whenever I did this with clients, like, I made it very adamant, like, you do not measure your body every two to three days. Like, that's, it's defeating, it doesn't even make sense. I know plenty of people that measure 15 and look eight, and many people that measure eight and look 15. And so, you're going after a look, you're going after a feeling, and just from a one-on-one basis, the machine can be used as a motivating tool for them, but doesn't really have much bearing on how you would operate from, like, a nutrition or training standpoint. So, can you gamify the system to their advantage? And if you can, then utilize that. Getting locked into the minutia is going to be frustrating and full of heartache. For both of you. All right. Next box. So, Alex, I've been in the industry 20 years now, seen a ton of trends come and go. 20 years ago, it was all one-on-one training, and the pendulum was all aesthetic based largely through the bodybuilding lens, right? And then functional fitness, group fitness came, swung all the way to the right. Seems like it's swinging back, and I see people like, I want to be functional, but I want to look good too. And obviously, the micro gym movement is here to stay. There's more boutiques than ever. I would just love to hear you talk from a macro level. Where do you see the industry going? And how, you know, are you positioning us to win in where the industry is going? And I know you had said, like, I don't know, last time, that one of your goals would be bigger than orange theory, and I love that. So we'd just love to hear some macro trends, visionary talk of where you see the industry going. Is that cool? All right. I'll try not to rant too long. So, I think that something that's really interesting from a marketing perspective, because I would also say that, like, challenges are something that have become really, really, really popular, and people have seen this, like, all over that fitness challenges are really big. I'll circle back to the answer. And I think the reason that that's happened is not that they wouldn't have been successful in the past. I think it's just that, given the vehicles that we have for buying attention, like direct mail, radio, we had such bigger buys that we had to do that we could only test limited options. And so we tested options and they worked good enough that that's what was being used. I have a hypothesis that if we had gone back in time and offered challenges, I think that we would have had even higher response rates. It's just that now with, like, internet marketing, we can do so many variations so quickly that we can optimize to truly find absolute best offers so much faster, that the offers today that we have are so far superior and the creative that we have today is so far superior because we test 100 options and find one winner, whereas before, you remember from the direct mail days, it's like, A.B., wait six months, A.B., wait six months, A.B., you know what I mean? Like, and we can do the same amount of testing in, like, three days. So that's just from an offer standpoint. From the macro level of, like, positioning us in the gym-wards community to win in the future market, I see having, the reason that I like the two-tiered model of service is that when, and this, I witnessed this with my facilities because the poorest facility that had was in La Habra, California. Has anyone been to La Habra? Yeah. Yeah, it's not, I mean, it's not the classiest place, but it's, you know, it's my style, it worked. But meeting income there was $31,000 a year for a household family in its California. So that gives you an idea. The gym that I had that was in the wealthiest area was in Lake Forest, California, and the median income there was $120,000. So big, big, big difference. And what happened is that with the two-tiered model, when in Lake Forest, there's way more people who will take that semi-private upsell so that it adapts to the marketplace that you're in. And in the cheaper market, I had a far bigger, large group and a very small, very small, small group, if that makes sense. So that depending on where we were in what market, the model adjusted itself. That being said, from an aesthetic to a performance standpoint, I think that if we see like a huge trend, like you see how quickly we adapt to things, if I feel like there's going to, if there's any kind of deficiency in terms of the deliverable on the fulfillment side, that's something that we can deploy our financial resources against making apps and things like that are specific to gym lords so people can track progress better from performance-based. If that's something that seems to be a huge desire in the marketplace. But I think that we're trying to stay humble enough to not say that we know anything at all, but just stay responsive to the marketplace. And if tomorrow all challenge ads stop working, there will be a new type of promotion that will work. And with the knowledge and the network that we now have access to, I can say that we have a high likelihood of, a high probability of being able to take any other winning offer and make it win within our model. Does that help for us? Was that useful? Yeah. Okay, cool. Next box. All right. This is confused, so the question's going to be off. I'm trying to wrap my head around not doing everything. First is what are some of your behaviors, habits that you use to manage your energy, your mindset, your confidence or certainty? And also the second thing is what are your personal beliefs and habits when it comes to managing your personal finances and also where you extend yourself and you reinvest within the business? Okay. So first question was about routine and habits for managing physical aspects and then the second question was about financial, how we manage money and things like that. From an energy standpoint, physically, the higher up we go in our business, the more we really like, Laila and I consume a ton of content about billionaires and what do they do, what are the things, what are the actions that they do. And the more that we see what you talked about attention is the more areas of your life you have under your control, the more attention you free up so that you can ultimately use it to grow whatever you want to grow. And so I now see training as a way for me to make more money. I see training as a way for me to serve you guys better. And I was actually talking to Dr. Kashi about this, is that I've actually found a new found motivation for me to train, which I've been training for 15 years hard, like hard. I had state records 10 years ago, that I'm more motivated now to train for you guys to stay in shape because I'm like, if I'm asking them to train, I should be training. And that's actually motivated me a ton from that aspect. For us, I've worked out in the middle of the day and done split shifts. I've done first thing in the morning and I've done afternoons. I wish I could give you one final answer. I think it's ultimately whatever you like and our schedules have changed and we've changed it. We just know that we're consistent with it. And then from a food aspect, everything that we do is always based on a goal. And I think that's just because that's who we are. And so I just like to always know that I'm making progress spiritually, financially, physically, whatever. And if I don't have that goal, that's where I start falling off. So I just like to always have a goal that I'm going towards because I have a fundamental belief that it's just as easy to lose weight, as maintain weight, as get stronger. It takes just as much time to build a $100,000 a year business as it does to build a $100 million a year business. It just takes different thoughts. I mean, they have the same time. It takes just as much time to build a $100,000 a year business as it does a $1 million or $10 million. That makes sense. And then as for the financial stuff, Layla and I have actually had tons of conversations about this with regards to debt. There are ways of leveraging debt to make more money. For us, our belief is that we got into entrepreneurship to be free. And so the question is, what's the cost benefit of me taking out leverage? Let's say we want to buy a $100 million building and we can put $10 million down. Let's say we want to do that. Then I feel like I would have just relinquished all the freedom that I had achieved to then do what? Make more money? To do what? Have more freedom? And so we asked ourselves that question, which was, do we really want to risk freedom to gain freedom? And so when we asked ourselves that question, that's why we fundamentally, like, our house is bought in cash, our cars are in cash, everything that we have is in cash. And it's very freeing. And it also allows us to operate from a place of abundance when we're making stuff for you guys. I don't feel rushed. You guys might feel that way. But I don't feel rushed in making something or releasing something because I need to make money or I need to do something. It's like I do it because I really want it to be the best. And so some of you guys know that there's a big thing that's on the horizon that we're working on. And it has been the hardest thing in the world for me to keep bottled in. But we're going to release it when it's right. And it allows us to operate from that standpoint. And then when I know that it does release, it's going to be the best thing that's ever been released for that. Does that help from a financial perspective? 100%. Thank you. And one more thing I'm going to tack on to my own answer. It's really, really, really, really helpful to have a game outside the game. Because right now, everyone is trying to win this one game. And then they take it out and then they just blow the money somewhere because you don't have a second game. The game of freedom is being able to increase your net worth sufficiently so that you can Dr. Cashew and I talk about this all the time, actually, is live on 4% of what you have. So whatever you can grab up as your nest egg, and the only ways to do that is either you can decrease how you live or you can increase what you have. But if you don't track it daily and Lissette, wherever she's at, she updates my numbers every single day, every single day. And it's the first thing I check on my phone is how much we have in every single account. And I'm kind of obsessive about it. But it's just because it's a marker of progress, not because this cost a bunch and then it goes down. But I just know what the trends are. And just focusing on it allows us to not make stupid decisions. That helped. Cool. I want to elaborate on the 4% really quick. How much money do you need to make per month to live? You can apply that by 25. And then that's how much you should have invested in any low to moderate risk venue. And then you can basically be in the position of F everybody. And make decisions based off of that. When it comes to, like, a lot of people ask, I don't know if this is kind of what I was listening to, but like, taking it and like, you know, investing and getting, leveraging the debt for real estate or something like that, like, it's all the flashy stuff out there. Like, it becomes its own business. And so you become in the business of managing money. Because doing that in itself is a job. And we like this business and we like what we do and we would rather serve people and make money that way than make money by like leveraging debt and like flipping whatever. Like, it's not fulfilling. And there's, it's really important, like, how you make your money. Because it's really not as fun when it comes from that rather than something that's fulfilling to you. Next box. Hello. Hello. So my wife and I started our gym about two and a half years ago. And during that time, we were so excited about opening a gym that we had two kids, or three kids, in about two years. And it kind of put her out. She was a huge influence when we opened the gym. She was probably a bigger influence than I was. And during these last two years, she was removed from the business. And this is the first opportunity that she's been able to come to an event like this and really see kind of behind the scenes of what's going on. And she's excited. She's like, I want to be part of this business again. So we had a conversation last night. And it was a little bit uncomfortable because I was like, you can do this. You can do this. She's like, I don't know. I don't want to do. I don't want to be a personal trainer. I don't want to work front desk. I was like, do whatever you want. And she's like, I need specific instructions. So I went to bed last night. And I woke up this morning, took a walk. And I created a four hours document. And I put the job title coincidentally as Mother Goose. So I created a four hours document. And my question is, how can we onboard somebody? Like how can I onboard my wife to a high position? Like number two in my company, successfully, without setting her up for success. So that she feels comfortable and I'm not pushing her too fast, that it doesn't ruin any kind of dynamic. Oh, dude. Yeah. I did this with Mimi because we had already, I think we had almost 20 people, maybe 16 actually, when we brought her on. And she's my sister. But she had been 10 years doing operational stuff. And she knew it before I knew anything about business. I was like, oh my gosh, this is so boring. I hate this crap. And she would talk operational, whatever. So as soon as I realized what I needed and I realized that she, hey, I was like, oh man, I want her to come here. And then once she agreed, it was like, well, how are we going to do this so that you're comfortable? And essentially, it just is going to take to move somebody not into a front line position, move them above others. And they're essentially inheriting a team. It's going to take a lot longer. So what happens is first, you two are going to lead side by side together. And so she's going to basically shadow you leading. And then within time, that's probably going to be for a month. And then within a couple more weeks, she's going to start leading some things. Like you start giving the smaller decisions over to her to give authority on. And you communicate this to the team. And then within, hopefully by three months, the end of three months, she can then take over all the decisions. But what you want to make sure you do is that you sit down and tell the team that this is what's going to happen. And this is the plan. And you want everyone to be on the same page so that they know. And then make them understand, tell them they're not going anywhere. You're still going to do this. These are still your roles. These are the things on her four R's. Share them with everyone. Explain it to everyone. Like I had a conversation with the team before Mimi came. And I was like, this is what she's going to do. This is how we're going to get her in. It's going to take a while. And when she came in, I was basically like, I have never onboarded somebody like this before. So I kind of want you to help me tell me each week and each day. So you have a lot of communication. What you need. Like what do you need to know? What do you need to see? So it would be like, Alex showing her all the flow of how a client comes in from here to here, to this page, this, and clicking through. And two hour Zoom calls on how the flow of the, once the sale gets in, where does the application go? What does it hook to? Where's our portal for this? So it's honestly just going to take a lot more time and a lot more intentional communication to the team so they understand what's going on too. And then it's going to be you edifying her that she is now in a place to make decisions. So the most important thing is that Alex did this to me. And then I do it to Mimi. It's like, they have earned this. I want you to understand why they have the expertise. So like this is why they have the expertise to do this. And then this is what they're going to be taking over. And so, and then you let them understand that now this person is the decision maker. And so you are the one that has to be clear to the team about that. And you can never, oops, and you can never cut in front of her or ever correct the decision that she makes in front of the team ever. Because the moment you undermine her, she disappears and then you're still the person who's making decisions. So then they ignore her. So you have to just let it happen. And if you do ever want to say something later, it has to be in the private. So make sure that she always has that. Like edifying is so huge. And then the other thing, I don't remember, but Mimi also shadowed each of our departments so that she could understand it. So she shadowed sales. She said, like, I would look at the five core functions and just be like, how do we generate leads? How does that work? How do we, how do we nurture them? What's our show up system? What do the sales look like? Shadowed on, you know, what does fulfillment look like? This is when they walk in the door. They have to check in, blah, blah, blah, blah. You know, like, here's fulfillment. Here's the challenge. Here's the clipboards, whatever. And then here's how we're retaining people. And so if you have the five core functions and she shadows the people who are in charge of each of those, she'll understand the entire flow of the business from front to end. And she'll get to know them at the same time. Yeah, both. Cool. Oh, yeah. This is a question for Alex and Layla. And a lot of the vegans that came in, like I think sometimes it's, they were the hardest. And so I would tell them, I'd be like, you guys are really hard to work with. I was just like really honest. And I was like, this is the best I can do. But like, there's past a certain point where I'm like, I'm not an expert in that. So like, if you want to lose fat, I know how these ways. I know like what you consider vegan because every vegan I've talked to has a different consideration of vegan or pescatarian or whatever. They all have different considerations or this affects my stomach or this, that, whatever. I would let them take some ownership over that for one. And then like some people, you should just turn away. Like there was people I would turn away because like, I was like, dude, like it's, I don't, you're not going to lose weight if you eat that much. So it just gives them like the base and then it just adds in another and each of the bulking plan is like first thing you can add adds 500 a day. If they don't gain on that, add the next thing, which is another 500 a day. They don't gain on that add another one, it's 500. So like by the end of it, they've added 2000 calories on top of the meal plan, which they should be gaining by then. I mean if you're eating a pound of pasta every day and you're taking two Gainer shakes, which are each like 1000 calories, like they're either lying or they're not gaining weight.
